data.title: CytomX Therapeutics: Updated Outlook For Late 2025
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

When I last covered CytomX Therapeutics (NASDAQ:CTMX), I maintained a bullish outlook after a then-recent run-up in market valuation based on favorable data tied to a cash raise from a position of strength. Today, I want to take another look to see about the ongoing developments of this company and their masked antibody-based immunotherapy approaches for cancer.

Pipeline Updates

CX-2051

CTMX is continuing its work in developing "probody" therapies, which are inactive antibodies that get processed near the tumor to become activated and recognize a target of interest, then delivering a chemotherapy payload. For CX-2051, that target is the EpCAM, which is expressed on a wide range of different cancer tissues.

In the last article, I detailed positive findings from an ongoing phase 1 trial focusing on patients with "refractory" colorectal cancer. Most notably, 17 out of 18 patients achieved some form of disease control with treatment using CX-2051. Diarrhea was common, and the rate of grade 3 diarrhea was 21.7%.

CTMX continues to guide that an update of this study, including around 70 patients, will come by Q1 2026, potentially putting it in the window of a data presentation at ASCO GI next January. They are also planning to initiate a phase 2 trial in advanced CRC in the first half of 2026.

CX-801

This probody targets IFN-alpha2b without delivering a chemotherapy payload. Rather, this molecule is intended to stimulate an antitumor immune response, with metastatic melanoma being the first target cancer of interest. A phase 1 dose escalation study is continuing to enroll patients, and CTMX has guided an initial data readout for Q4 2025.

Back in May, CTMX initiated a phase 1 study combining CX-801 with Merck's pembrolizumab for patients with metastatic melanoma and disease that is refractory to anti-PD-1 therapy.

Financial Overview

As of their most recent quarterly filing, CTMX held $49.0 million in cash and equivalents, along with $109 million in short-term investments. They recognized $18.7 million in revenue related to their various ongoing collaborations, mostly Bristol Myers Squibb, Astellas, and Regeneron. Meanwhile, operating expenses reached $19.9 million, and after interest, the net loss for the quarter was $0.15 million.

The net cash used for the first 6 months in 2025 was $36.8 million. At this burn rate, CTMX has between a little over 2 years of funding to continue operations, consistent with management guidance that their expected cash runway is into Q2 2027.

Strengths and Risks

Strength - Strong liquidity to reach some important data catalysts

With 2 years of funding in the tank, CTMX has a good chance to reach important clinical milestones that could force the market to re-evaluate the valuation. The multitude of collaborations that are ongoing are helping to offset the costs of a burgeoning, nascent pipeline, as well.

Risk - Probodies and masked immunotherapies in general still have a lot of proving to do

This is the big question mark for the entire field of masked immunotherapies. To date, we've gotten a few blips suggesting that tolerability and efficacy can be improved, and that challenging targets like EGFR can be exploited relatively safely (something that has not been possible with immunotherapy to date).

What we've seen so far coming from CTMX, in particular, does offer some encouragement. And colorectal cancer remains a strong unmet need, even if Exelixis makes good on transforming the standard of care (see my recent article on STELLAR-303 if you want to learn more about that). However, the space of refractory colorectal cancer is challenging for drug development, and we will need to watch closely whether the signals of efficacy are getting stronger.

Strength - Numerous preclinical partnerships that don't yet register on the clinical pipeline could mean positive surprises

Since I normally cover only the clinical candidate projects of these biotechs, it's easy to discount the rather large number of big pharma partnerships that CTMX has managed to wrangle. At any time, these probodies and other undisclosed preclinical projects could ascend to the clinical pipeline and become a force to be reckoned with, lending further upside to the CTMX investment thesis.

Bottom-Line Summary

At the time of writing, CTMX continues to be valued at a premium relative to their liquidity ($330 million market cap compared with ~$140 million in liquidity). So the market sees value in the pipeline, which is far from a guarantee in this day and age of developmental biotech.

Still, the company's valuation has remained fairly flat since their initial disclosure of promising colorectal cancer data earlier this year. And I continue to see strong upside potential if they can continue to build on that momentum. Based on the expectation of other important data catalysts between now and early next year, I think it is reasonable to see this valuation as a potential floor, and the equity continues to be worthy of consideration in a risk-tolerant portfolio.

There could be a better entry point into the stock, but if CTMX's 70-patient colorectal cancer data continue to impress, this will likely be a catalyst for substantial appreciation in market sentiment. This is definitely one worth following closely.

data.title: Assembly Biosciences: Positive Data Of ABI-5366 Brings 2 Fall Program Catalysts
data.description:
Today, Assembly Biosciences, Inc. (NASDAQ:ASMB) released results from its phase 1b study using its long-acting herpes simplex virus [HSV] helicase-primase inhibitor ABI-5366 for the treatment of patients with recurrent genital herpes. However, right away it is important to note that the goal is to target such recurrent herpes patients who are specifically seropositive for HSV type 2 [HSV-2]. The use of this drug is being evaluated in the ongoing phase 1b study targeting this specific group of HSV patients.

Interim data was revealed, but why do I believe that there is further value that can be obtained through the development of this specific program? It is because the positive interim data released from this phase 1b study using ABI-5366 brings about another batch of catalysts that are expected in the fall of 2025.

The interim data that was released from this phase 1b trial dealt with weekly dosing of this particular drug. Thus, the next catalyst to come about in the fall of 2025 would be the release of monthly data from this trial targeting these seropositive for HSV2 recurrent genital herpes patients. That's not all because the company also has another long-acting HSV helicase-primase inhibitor it is developing to treat these HSV-2 recurrent genital herpes patients, which is known as ABI-1179.

One thing to note is that this specific candidate was obtained as part of a collaboration between it and Gilead Sciences (GILD). Speaking of this ABI-1179 candidate, this is also being explored in a phase 1b study targeting the same group of patients and data from it is also expected to be released in the fall of 2025. Furthermore, positive data obtained from both of these phase 1b studies have the power for further shareholder value creation. This would be the fact that upon delivering the final phase 1b data packages to Gilead, this pharma would have the option to opt in for further development. If it decides to do this, then I believe this is another milestone that could cause Assembly Biosciences' stock to trade higher.

Two Shots On Goal In The Targeting Of Seropositive HSV-2 Recurrent Genital Herpes Patients

As I noted above, Assembly Biosciences is in the process of advancing two clinical candidates for the treatment of patients with HSV-2 recurrent genital herpes. These include long-acting HSV helicase-primase inhibitors of ABI-5366 and ABI-1179. Because the company released interim data with that of ABI-5366 targeting this specific groups of genital herpes patients, this is the program I'm going to go over below.

However, just know that this biotech does have another shot on goal with the development of the other ABI-1179 candidate I just mentioned. It was revealed today that the company achieved positive interim data from the phase 1b study, using ABI-5366 for the treatment of patients with HSV-2 recurrent genital herpes. Before going over the release of this specific interim results released by it, plus what catalysts are to come about from this entire program, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Genital Herpes is characterized as a common sexually transmitted infection [STI] caused by the herpes simplex virus [HSV]. The truth is that this is quite annoying and painful for a patient. Why do I state that? Well, it is because if a person has this they generate painful sores and blisters in multiple parts of their body, such as buttocks, genitals, and/or inner thighs. What's even worse is that there is no cure, the only thing that current medications do is to stop the spread of it [manage the outbreak itself] and also to treat some symptoms.

The global herpes simplex virus treatment market is expected to reach $4.08 billion by 2030. The next part is important because it deals with the specifics of Assembly Biosciences focusing on a specific patient population. Genital herpes or herpes simplex virus is caused by HSV-1 and HSV-2. The company is developing ABI-5366 [along with ABI-1179] to specifically target seropositive for HSV-2 genital herpes patients. A further focus is emphasized on targeting such patients with recurrent disease [recurrent genital herpes].

The targeting of HSV-2 patients is ideal, because this is more common for starters. In addition, it is also important to note that HSV-2 genital herpes patient are far more prone to recurrences. As a matter of fact, it is said that they have 3 or more recurrences per year. If that wasn't bad enough, it gets worse for these patients. The current standard of care [SOC] treatment option for these patients are nucleoside analogs. They are okay, but the problem is that they are only partially effective. In essence, they don't prevent recurrences all that well.

Where does Assembly have a chance with either of these drugs ABI-5366 and/or ABI-1179? It is in the fact that there have been no new approved drugs in either the United States or Europe in over 25 years. This means, there is an unmet medical need that both it and its partner Gilead Sciences could possibly help with. Of course, that's only if positive data is obtained in the ongoing phase 1b studies and the soon to possibly be initiated phase 2 studies using both of these long-acting HSV helicase-primase inhibitors for these seropositive for HSV-2 recurrent genital herpes patients.

In order to see whether or not ABI-5366 is going to be effective in treating these HSV-2 recurrent genital herpes patients, it is in the process of running the ongoing phase 1b study. This early-stage trial is expected to recruit up to a total of 146 patients who are to be randomized to receive either 30 mg or 350 mg of drug or placebo. This is to be noted as follows:

  • Cohort B1 - Loading dose of 150 mg of ABI-5366 with 30 mg of weekly oral dosing.
  • Cohort B2 - Loading dose of 350 mg of ABI-5366 with 350 mg of weekly oral dosing.

This is what has been revealed with the interim data I'm about to go over so far. The company has already begun testing Cohort B3, which is another crucial component for this program, because it is evaluating the once-monthly dosing of ABI-5366. The goal was to evaluate two efficacy endpoints as part of this early-stage phase 1b trial, which were HSV-2 shedding rate and genital lesion rate. The ultimate goal was to achieve a statistically significant reduction of both of these endpoint measures of ABI-5366 versus placebo. Both of these efficacy endpoints were met in a statistically significant manner with a p-value of p<0.01. It was proven that patients who took this drug once a week had achieved a % reduction rate of HSV-2 shedding rate and genital lesion rate of 94% [both same outcome].

Forging ahead, this company is in good shape because it has already been able to complete toxicology studies that are to support long-term dosing of ABI-5366 to target these HSV-2 recurrent genital herpes patients. The downside is that long-term phase 2 dosing to test out this particular drug to treat these patients is not expected to start until mid-2026.

This catalyst is far away for sure, but that doesn't mean that there aren't other catalysts that investors can look forward to in the 2nd half of this year. For starters, it goes back to what I noted in the beginning above, which is that the company expects to release monthly dosing data of ABI-5366 from cohort 3B of the ongoing phase 1b study in the fall of 2025. This, along with the expected data to be released from the other ongoing phase 1b study, uses ABI-1179 to target this group of HSV-2 recurrent genital herpes patients.

Besides this catalyst, this program is ideal for one other major reason, and that is the potential for further advancement with its partner Gilead Sciences. Specifically, upon this pharmaceutical company receiving option data packages for both ABI-5366 and ABI-1179 of the phase 1b study program, it will have the ability to opt-in for further development. This makes sense based on the way that this collaboration agreement was made, which is that each opt-in Gilead makes results in Assembly Biosciences being able to receive a $45 million payment. Plus, the $100 million upfront [$85 million in cash and then 15% as an equity investment] it already received as part of forming this collaboration agreement with respect to the entire helicase-primase inhibitor program.

The thing is that this collaboration also involves the development of other antiviral programs as well, like developing candidates for Hepatitis B Virus [HBV] and Hepatitis Delta Virus [HDV].

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC filing, Assembly Biosciences had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $75 million as of June 30, 2025. The company doesn't have a lot of cash on hand, and there is even more bad news than this. What would this be? Well, it notes that it believes that with the ongoing candidates it is advancing, it is likely to see an increase in expenses going forward.

More specifically, it noted in its 10-Q SEC filing that it believed it would have enough funds for a cash runway to fund its operations into mid-2026. The truth is that this was not a lot of cash on hand, and it would have likely needed to raise funds either by the end of 2025 or in the early part of 2026. With that being said, just today, it enacted the pricing of a $175 million equity financing. This is expected to close on or before August 11, 2025.

The company burns roughly $20.7 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $16.1 million in R&D expenses and then $4.6 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Assembly Biosciences. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the recent release of the interim data from the phase 1b study using ABI-5366 to treat patients with HSV-2 recurrent genital herpes. The company had only released once-weekly oral dosing of this drug as part of the interim data release. What this means is that it is expected that monthly dosing of ABI-5366 is to be released in the fall of 2025.

The risk here is that even though the interim data released just today showed a percentage reduction of HSV-2 shedding rate and genital lesion rate of 94%, there is no assurance that the next expected monthly dosing data is going to end up being positive. Lastly, it is important to highlight that the placebo portion of patients is pooled across all cohorts. What this means is that as additional placebo patients are added, the efficacy values could adjust and/or change later on.

The second risk deals with the other long-acting HSV-2 HSV helicase-primase inhibitor ABI-1179 for the treatment of patients with HSV-2 recurrent genital herpes in a phase 1b study. The goal is to have interim data released from this particular study in the fall of 2025. The risk here is that even though ABI-5366 was shown to have achieved a high percentage reduction of HSV-2 shedding rate and lesion rate in a statistically significant manner, there is no assurance that this other long-acting oral candidate is going to achieve a similar or superior outcome.

The third risk to consider would be in terms of all of Assembly Biosciences' ongoing antiviral program candidates from its pipeline. That's because several of these candidates, like ABI-5366, ABI-1179 and next-generation capsid assembly modulator [CAM] ABI-4334 and beyond, are being done with the potential for a Gilead Sciences opt in. This means that upon completion of several phase 1b studies, this pharma will decide if it wants to opt-in for further advancement based on option data packages it receives.

The risk here is that despite good interim data being obtained in the ongoing phase 1b study of ABI-5366 for the treatment of patients with HSV-2 recurrent genital herpes, there is no guarantee that Gilead Sciences will decide to opt-in to this specific program for further advancement. In addition, there is no assurance that it will ultimately decide to opt-in to the other programs being developed as part of the collaboration agreement it had established with Assembly.

The fourth and final risk to consider would be in terms of the development of ABI-4334 as a next-generation capsid assembly modulator [CAM] to treat patients with Hepatitis B Virus [HBV]. The company recently announced data from the phase 1b study using this candidate to treat this patient population. I noted that it would have the flexibility to use the lower dose of 150 mg of drug and the potential to increase to higher dosing of it in order to maximize the inhibition of cccDNA.

The risk here is that there is no assurance that moving to a higher dose is going to result in an increase of inhibiting cccDNA. Not only that, but should Gilead choose to opt-in and advance this to phase 2 testing, the company notes that it will have to establish combination regimens along with it. The risk is that there is no assurance that ABI-4334 evaluated in combination with another drug is going to result in increased mean plasma DNA reductions compared to what has been established for this drug alone [Mean plasma HBV DNA reductions of 3.2 logs IU/mL over 28 days for 400 mg].

Conclusion

Assembly Biosciences has done well to continue to develop its long-acting HSV helicase-primase inhibitor ABI-5366 for the treatment of patients with HSV-2 recurrent genital herpes. What's so good about this program is the proof-of-concept it has provided that its drug works in treating this group of patients when given at the highest dose once weekly. What remains to be seen, though, is whether or not the once-monthly dosing being explored in Cohort 3B of the phase 1 study using ABI-5366 to treat these patients is going to achieve a similar or superior outcome.

The beauty of this biotech is that it has another shot on goal in targeting the very large global herpes simplex virus. As I have stated above, this is expected to be a large multibillion-dollar market by 2030. Another program it has going to provide shareholder value would be the evaluation of ABI-4334 as a next-generation capsid assembly modulator [CAM] to treat patients with HBV. Along with the other hepatitis delta virus [HDV] candidate ABI-6250 as well. With positive data from the phase 1b study using ABI-5366 to treat patients with serotype of HSV-2 recurrent genital herpes, plus the ability to see once-monthly dosing data and phase 1b ABI-1179 data in the fall of 2025, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains to be made here.

data.title: Duolingo Q2: The Price Level I Load Up At (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Duolingo (NASDAQ:DUOL) in June, I reiterated my “hold” rating on the stock, as I believed that investor optimism on the stock had gotten far too extended with the stock price exceeding free cash flow growth, thus leaving little room for error.

Since then, the stock is down over 25%, underperforming the S&P 500.

Duolingo recently reported its Q2 FY25 earnings, where the stock jumped close to 20% as revenue growth accelerated, while Adjusted EBITDA margins expanded both on a year-over-year and sequential basis from superior user monetization.

During the earnings call, the management discussed that Chess has grown to surpass the number of DAUs (Daily Active Users) when compared to its Math and Music offering, while its recently launched Energy feature is showcasing promise in driving user growth and conversion on the platform.

For the full year, the management also raised their revenue guidance as well as their expectation for Adjusted EBITDA margin.

The way I see it, with the stock shedding its excessive weight, it has now finally allowed an attractive entry point for long-term investors. As a result, I will be upgrading the stock to a “buy” with a price target of $480, representing an upside of 26% from its current levels.

Expanding DAUs as Duolingo sees success with Chess along with its newly released Energy feature

Duolingo reported its Q2 FY25 earnings this week, where revenue growth accelerated during the quarter to $252.3M, up 41% YoY and beating estimates by over 4%, with Subscription Revenue growing 46% YoY and contributing to over 83% of Total Revenue, 300 basis points higher than last year.

During the quarter, the company saw its DAUs grow 40% YoY to 47.7M. Aside from their social-first marketing strategy to grow user engagement, the company continued to ship dozens of rewarding interactions to make the app more fun and enjoyable, such as the lightning bolt when a user successfully completes a lesson or their in-app character Eddy triumphantly flexing when a user is able to solve a hard exercise.

On top of that, the company’s Chess course has been growing faster than any other subject, with over a million DAUs as of quarter end, surpassing Maths and Music that they added last year. During the earnings call, the management sounded confident that they would be able to attract new audiences and deepen engagement with existing Chess users by designing better content and creating gamification strategies to drive superior engagement in matches against users.

Finally, Duolingo introduced a core feature called Energy, which is usage-based and rewards successes, with each exercise utilizing a unit of energy regardless of whether the answer is correct or not, with users getting back “energy” when they get multiple answers correct in a row.

There is no doubt that Duolingo is a master when it comes to building gamification strategies to improve user experiences. In this case, Energy is designed for free users only, with promising initial results, including increasing DAUs, median time spent on the app, and subscriber conversion.

No slowdown in user monetization in sight, though regulatory hurdles in China remain a headwind for Max rollout.

The main driver behind Duolingo’s revenue acceleration during the quarter was its deepening user monetization, with Paid Subscribers as a percentage of LTM (Last 12 Months) MAUs (Monthly Active Users) growing both on a year-over-year and sequential basis to 9.0%, with Paid Subscribers growing 37% YoY.

During the quarter, the company saw its subscription growth in its Super tier exceed expectations, while its GenAI-powered Max tier also saw significant traction.

To remind readers, Duolingo offers three subscription tiers that include freemium, Duolingo Super, and Duolingo Max, with both Super and Max offering Family Plan options.

While CEO Luis Alfonso von Ahn Arellano discussed risks of regulatory hurdles that may delay the rollout in China that may continue to delay Max rollout in the region, the management still raised their forward guidance.

Lower than anticipated headwind from AI costs impacting profitability

Shifting gears to profitability, Duolingo was able to expand its gross margins by 130 basis points sequentially to 72.4%, although it declined 100 basis points year-over-year due to higher AI costs associated with the expansion of Max.

Still, it was less than the 300 basis points shrinkage on a year-over-year basis that Duolingo management was anticipating.

Meanwhile, the company was able to expand its operating leverage given superior user monetization, resulting in Q2 Adjusted EBITDA growing over 63% YoY to $78.7M, with margins increasing both on a year-over-year and sequential basis to 31.2%.

Management raised guidance, and valuation suggests upside from current levels.

For FY25, Duolingo management has raised guidance, where they now expect full-year revenues to grow 35.2%-35.2% YoY to $1.011-$1.019B. This is compared to their previous guidance of expecting revenue growth in the range of 31.9-33.2% YoY for the full year.

Meanwhile, when it comes to profitability, the management now expects gross margins to decline by 100 basis points year-over-year, compared to their previous guidance of a 150 basis point headwind, while raising their guidance for Adjusted EBITDA margin to 28.5-29% (vs. their previous guidance of 27.5-28.5%).

Looking forward, I will take management’s FY25 revenue guidance into account while taking forward consensus estimates for the years after, where Duolingo is expected to grow its revenues in the mid- to high-twenties range to $1.59B by FY27.

From a profitability standpoint, I expect that the company will be able to unlock at least 300 basis points of operating leverage every year into FY27 as gross margins normalize and returns back in the 73% range, while the company is able to successfully drive superior user monetization by deepening the penetration of its Super and Max subscription tiers.

This will translate to an Adjusted EBITDA of $557M by FY27, which will be equivalent to a present value of $460M when discounted at 8%.

Assuming a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 48, which is roughly 2.5 times that of the average S&P 500 forward price-to-earnings multiple, given the growth rate of its earnings during this period of time, we arrive at a price target of $480 per share, representing an upside of 26% from its current levels.

My final verdict and conclusions

With the stock shedding its excessive weight while strengthening its underlying fundamentals with growing user monetization and management raising full-year guidance for both top and bottom lines, I believe an opportunity has finally presented itself for investors to initiate a long-term position in the company.

As a result, I am upgrading my rating to a “buy” with a price target of $480, representing an upside of 26% from its current levels.

data.title: Wolfspeed Hold Rating: Debt, Cash Drain, And Bankruptcy Keep The Future In Doubt
data.description:
Wolfspeed, Inc. (NSYE:WOLF) is living through a make-or-break moment. At today’s price, the market clearly expects the worst and I completely get why. The company’s debt is massive, the cash burn is intense and there is a bankruptcy process already in motion. At the same time, Wolfspeed is not just another chip company with a generic product. The tech here actually matters for things like electric vehicles and renewables, which gives it at least a fighting chance to survive this mess, if everything lines up just right.

Right now, I don’t see a strong reason to buy or to sell. The risk of losing it all is very real for anyone holding the stock but on the flip side, the upside is only possible if the restructuring actually works and Wolfspeed manages to fix its business without completely crushing shareholders. For most investors, I believe the right call is to stay put and watch how this drama unfolds. Jumping in fresh would be rolling the dice and selling out here feels a bit like throwing in the towel at the bottom, unless you simply can’t stomach the risk.

What Wolfspeed Does and Where It Stands

Wolfspeed calls itself a leader in silicon carbide and gallium nitride chips and to be fair, its products have a real role in the next wave of electric vehicles, fast-charging stations and some power applications that regular silicon just can’t handle. The company’s roots run deep and it has tried to create a special place in the chip world. But none of that shields it from the situation it faces now.

The truth is, Wolfspeed is in survival mode. After taking on more than $6 billion in debt and burning through its cash, it had to throw in the towel and file for bankruptcy protection earlier this summer. There have been a lot of leadership changes, including new people in the CFO and COO seats who seem to have one job: keep the company alive and get the finances under control. Wolfspeed did sell its RF business to make things more manageable but it is still a complicated business to run. Right now, it is less about beating the competition with new ideas and more about surviving.

The Market Wolfspeed Is In

If you step back, the industry Wolfspeed operates in is actually exciting. Silicon carbide and gallium nitride are the future for a lot of power electronics. Big names like Infineon (OTCQX:IFNNY) and ON Semiconductor (ON) have noticed this too and are building out their own production lines. That means Wolfspeed is not the only one racing to win contracts for the next round of electric vehicles and clean energy projects. There are also big swings in revenue depending on when customers decide to upgrade their tech or start new projects, which makes things even bumpier.

While government support like the CHIPS Act has given the whole sector a boost, not every company is built to last. Wolfspeed’s technology is valuable but these days, the ability to raise cash and keep the lights on is just as important as having a cool product.

How the Numbers Look

The financial story here is about as rough as it gets. Wolfspeed brought in $185 million in revenue for the most recent quarter, which is down compared to last year. Margins are deep in the red, with gross losses and operating losses both piling up. The company lost almost $285 million in just three months and close to a billion dollars over the past nine months.

Cash is disappearing quickly, dropping from $2.2 billion last summer to about $1.3 billion in March, even as debt sits above $6.5 billion. The latest numbers also show that shareholders’ equity has been slashed to just above $200 million. The company itself admits that there is “serious doubt” about its ability to keep going unless the restructuring works. If you are looking for any sort of financial cushion, you won’t find it here.

Is the Stock Cheap or Not?

It’s tough to use traditional valuation math with Wolfspeed. Sure, the price is slightly above book value but the company is burning so much cash that book value could shrink even more or get wiped out entirely. Forward earnings estimates are deeply negative for the next couple of years, so things like price-to-earnings are meaningless right now. Even the company’s market value, which is down to $234 million, looks tiny next to the mountain of debt it owes. The enterprise value is over seven times sales, which would be rich for a healthy business, never mind one in the middle of bankruptcy. Even after falling over 90 percent, I just don’t see a clear reason to call Wolfspeed a bargain.

What the News and Market Say

If you’ve followed the headlines, you know how wild it’s been. The restructuring news at the end of June caused a brief spike in the stock price but it didn’t last. Stories about bankruptcy, lawsuits and funds selling their shares have taken over the news feed. Leadership changes are coming fast but it feels like crisis management, not a turnaround. From where I sit, the market expects disaster and has already priced in a lot of pain. The only folks still in the game are speculators looking to make a quick buck on big swings.

The Big Risks to Watch

Let’s be honest: the risks are huge. Bankruptcy could wipe out existing shareholders or dilute them to the point that even a rebound doesn’t help much. The cash burn is out of control and with creditors now calling the shots, shareholders have very little power. Bigger companies are making moves in silicon carbide and Wolfspeed is playing catch-up with less cash and more distractions. Legal fights, class actions and the need to raise new money all make things worse. Any talk of a buyout or “too important to fail” scenario is more wishful thinking than an actual plan at this stage.

What Could Happen Next

For the next six months, everything depends on how the restructuring turns out. The company says it can keep running for the next year but that assumes it can pull off asset sales and get creditors to play ball. If Wolfspeed makes it through this period, there is still a long-term market for its products. The problem is that analyst estimates show the company losing money for years to come, so there’s no quick fix in sight. Operational improvements will take time and even then, the company has to convince customers and investors that it’s here to stay.

Different Paths from Here

When I look at possible futures, the worst-case scenario is clear: the restructuring fails, creditors take over and shareholders end up with nothing or next to nothing. The most likely outcome, in my opinion, is a severely diluted turnaround where the company limps along and maybe, just maybe, claws its way back over time. There’s also a chance, slim as it is, that Wolfspeed pulls off a miracle, manages the restructuring, keeps customers and wins new business, all while raising enough money to survive. In that best case, the stock could bounce but any rally would likely be from a much lower base after all the dust settles.

My Bottom Line

To wrap it up, I see Wolfspeed as a textbook example of a “wait and see” story. The market has already given up hope but that doesn’t make the stock a steal. With the bankruptcy process and debt overhang, there’s a real risk of losing your entire investment. At the same time, it feels too late to panic and sell unless you just want to move on. If the company manages to shed its debt and rebuild, maybe there’s a shot at a turnaround but I wouldn’t rush in now. Personally, I’d keep watching from the sidelines.

data.title: Q2 Earnings: BTC Gains Trump Rising Costs For Hut 8
data.description:
As we approach five months since my last Seeking Alpha article covering Hut 8 Corp. (NASDAQ:HUT), we have two additional quarters of company performance to assess in addition to the performance of the stock price. Readers might recall that back in March I felt investors would do better being long Bitcoin (BTC-USD) rather than HUT shares:

While I'm encouraged by Hut 8's ability to transform the business over the last year or two, the equity still goes as Bitcoin goes. And if given a choice between the two, investors will likely do much better just buying Bitcoin.

As fate would have it, that call has turned out to be wrong:

Since my last HUT piece, the stock - as well as the broader mining sector - outperformed Bitcoin by a rather significant margin. I think it's worth looking at HUT's recent earnings report to estimate whether this out-performance can reasonably continue.

Q2-25 Earnings

For quarter-ended June 2025, Hut 8 reported $41.3 million in top line revenue. This was a 17.3% year over year increase from June 2024. By segment, the revenue breakout was as follows:

Revenue 000s 3mo 6/30/25 3mo 6/30/24 YOY
Power $5,492 $10,530 -47.8%
Digital Infrastructure $1,512 $5,264 -71.3%
Compute $34,295 $15,795 117.1%
Other $0 $3,626 -100.0%
Total Revenue $41,299 $35,215 17.3%

Source: Hut 8.

These numbers are a bit all over the place due to Hut 8's reclassification and consolidation of certain revenue segments. Underneath the "Power" umbrella, Hut 8 generates revenue from capacity contracts and energy sales to the Ontario electrical grid. Under this same segment, Hut 8 offers its energy infrastructure expertise to institutional clients. Within Hut 8's "Digital Infrastructure" segment, the company offers co-location services for both Bitcoin mining and CPU-based workloads. "Compute" rolls up three different sub-segments that include Bitcoin mining - which has been spun out to American Bitcoin - data center cloud services, and Highrise AI; the latter of which runs a GPU-as-a-service model.

As would likely be expected given the operations of the legacy Hut 8 entity and the biggest contributor to top line revenue in the table above, Compute was Hut's largest expense with $14.6 million in COGS in quarter-ended June.

COGS 000s 3mo 6/30/25 3mo 6/30/24 YOY
Power $5,000 $5,449 -8.2%
Digital Infrastructure $2,120 $4,331 -51.1%
Compute $14,656 $8,670 69.0%
Other $0 $2,186 -100.0%
Total COGS $21,776 $20,636 5.5%

Source: Hut 8.

Compute COGS grew year over year by 69%, which was actually quite a bit less than the 117% year over year growth in revenue from that category. This led to a monster gain in gross profit for Compute from $7.1 million last year to $19.6 million this year. That said, the year over year performance in the other segments looks quite rough:

Gross Profit 000s 3mo 6/30/25 3mo 6/30/24 YOY
Power $492 $5,081 -90.3%
Digital Infrastructure -$608 $933 -165.2%
Compute $19,639 $7,125 175.6%
Other $0 $1,440 -100.0%
Gross Profit $19,523 $14,579 33.9%

Source: Hut 8.

Again, this is due to segment consolidation and reclassification to some degree. But gross profit in Power fell by 90% year over year and Digital Infrastructure turned negative. This was largely impacted by the termination of an agreement with Ionic Digital. Compute remains the biggest driver of the top line for Hut 8 and that segment is still highly reliant on Bitcoin mining. As has been a common tale in mining earnings reports for the last few quarters, positive net income is generally only possible with paper gains in Bitcoin holdings.

During the quarter, Hut 8 reported a positive net income of $137.5 million. Take away the $217.6 million gain on BTC holdings and Hut 8's real operations were actually negative to the tune of $80.2 million in the quarter. I recognize that FASB accounting rules allow for this, but I do think it can be misleading to include unrealized gains on dormant assets in a company's operational performance as it can potentially give investors a false view of the company's true profitability. And if BTC asset revaluation is going to be the key determinant in Hut's corporate performance, then the level of BTC relative to the market valuation of the equity may be important to consider.

mNAV, Sats Per Share, and Ownership

While HUT is not a 'treasury company' in the same sense that an entity like Strategy (MSTR) would be considered one, Hut 8 does command the 12th largest BTC-stack held by any public company with 10,667 BTC as of quarter-ended June.

Given the company's current market value, HUT has an mNAV of 1.76. This is close to the mNAV of Strategy and generally in-line with other mining stocks like Riot Platforms (RIOT) and CleanSpark (CLSK). Even as the company has often relied on shareholder dilution to fund growth, the year over year change in the "sats per share" figure shown below has grown 2% year over year from 10,017 to 10,217.

This is well below the YoY sat-backing growth that we've seen from companies like MARA Holdings (MARA) or Riot Platforms but ahead of similarly sized Cipher Mining (CIFR). It should also be noted that recent "sats per share" numbers over the last few quarters have been roughly flat and well below the highs from 2022.

Something else that I like to look at with the public miners is the degree to which hedge funds are holding the stocks. As of Q1 25, 30% of HUT's outstanding shares are held by the "smart money."

This 30% hedge fund ownership generally puts HUT in the middle of the pack relative to the 12 stocks shown above, but much closer to top-owned Core Scientific (CORZ) than HIVE Digital Technologies (HIVE). My read on this is that even though Hut 8 is seen as one of the stronger bets in the public mining space by fund managers.

Risk To Consider

We've already seen Hut is not a profitable company without BTC-revaluation. Despite that, Compute has actually seen growth in gross margins year over year. From where I sit, a big factor holding back Hut from positive net income in more real terms are the ballooning expenses in SG&A and Depreciation & Amortization:

Both categories have seen growth outpace total revenue over the last year. But it's the level to which the spending is occurring relative to top line revenue that I find more concerning. In Q2 24, SG&A was 44.5% of revenue - which is still quite high. That figure ballooned to 89.7% in Q1-25 and 73% last quarter. While lower on a relative basis, Depreciation and Amortization came in at 68.3% and 47.1% of revenue over the last two quarters. To be clear, Hut is not the worst offender of SG&A as a percentage of revenue, but the trend is worrisome if the HPC/AI data center build out turns out to be a bust.

Closing Takeaways

I think the major concern here is fairly straightforward; it takes unrealized gains in Bitcoins that have already been mined to generate positive net income in the reporting quarter. This has the double impact of masking operational losses while also putting the company at significant risk if the value of that Bitcoin were to actually go down substantially - as has been the case numerous times over the coins's nearly 16 year history.

There are likely some who view Bitcoin cycle theory as less relevant in a post-ETF world, but Bitcoin the asset is currently reliant on capital flows rather than underlying usage of the network itself. As easily as this capital has moved into BTC, it can most assuredly move out as well. That said, Bitcoin's current trajectory still appears to be up. If the company can continue to grow "sats per share" the way it has over the last quarter, then it stands to reason that HUT could theoretically continue to outperform BTC in a bull run. As a raw BTC-proxy, I like other opportunities better. And as an HPC/AI investment, there are other companies that are closer to pure play. I'm still going to rate HUT shares a Hold today.

data.title: EWA: A Guide To This Australian Large-Cap ETF
data.description:
Introduction To The iShares MSCI Australia ETF

The iShares MSCI Australia ETF (NYSEARCA:EWA), which is backed by Blackrock, Inc., is a $1.5 billion-sized product that covers roughly 50 stocks from the Australian equity market. EWA, which will complete three decades as a listed product in March 2026, offers a solid enough yield of over 3% and can be picked up at an expense ratio of 0.50%.

What Does EWA Do?

Principally, EWA tracks the MSCI Australia Index (MAI), which is designed to serve as a barometer of Australian equities for foreign investors. The index aims to cover 85% of the total free-float market capitalization of the Australian markets.

Ewa tracks this index through representative sampling. This means EWA won’t necessarily own every single stock that MAI covers, and also not in the same proportion. Rather, it will only choose to cover a certain sample of stocks that, in aggregate, end up having similar features as the index. These features include the average market cap, the risk profile, the fundamental characteristics, the economic characteristics, etc. Also, EWA typically only sets out to ensure that 80% of its assets include the component equities of MAI; the remaining 20% could even include non-MAI equities, but also some derivative products and money-market funds.

Usually passive ETFs that follow the representative sampling strategy (rather than fully replicating the holdings of their underlying index) will typically tend to post rather wide tracking errors. The tracking error basically gives you a sense of how different an ETF's returns are relative to the returns of its underlying index. In EWA’s case, we can see that the tracking error has widened over the past year (and is well above the ETF median error rates of 7.4%), compared to what was seen over a three-year duration (roughly in line with the ETF median).

What Are The Main Characteristics Of EWA’s Portfolio?

Even though EWA’s tracking index is believed to cover 85% of the Australian equities market cap (on a free-float basis), since it assigns portfolio weights on the respective market cap of the stocks (larger market caps garner higher weights), it is heavily tilted towards giant and large-cap Australian stocks, which account for a whopping ~96% of the total portfolio. While mid-caps account for a marginal proportion, Aussie small-caps don’t get any representation.

Compounding the giant-cap tilt, we also have a scenario where 10 stocks alone, or roughly one-fifth of the portfolio (in terms of numbers), take up a hefty weight of 63% in aggregate, or close to two-thirds of the portfolio in value terms.

There is a very clear sector concentration effect in play with financial stocks dominating this portfolio: 5 out of the top 10 stocks from this portfolio are financials, while over 40% of the portfolio comes from this sector alone.

Who Is EWA Meant For?

EWA is meant for investors who are looking for concentrated exposure to Australian giant caps and are prepared to overlook the seemingly alluring growth runway that one would typically associate with mid-caps and small caps. These mid-caps may be intrinsically riskier in their movements (relative to giant-caps), but a lot of them are still closer to their infancy and likely far from hitting a saturation point in their overall development lifecycle. An overexposure to Australian giant-caps will also imply a predilection towards more diversified business models that aren’t strictly reliant on prospects in the domestic economy alone and are also closely impacted by developments across the world.

Investors who are looking to own a stable basket of stocks for the long haul will also likely gravitate to EWA because it is not a portfolio that undertakes a great deal of churn on an annual basis. Most ETFs turnover around 30% of their portfolio every year, but in EWA’s just a minuscule component of 5% gets turned over.

EWA would also appeal to someone who is having a hard time choosing between large-cap growth and value-style stocks, as both these buckets have an equal weight, with blended large-caps (those that have both growth and value characteristics taking up the largest share) dominating.

For the uninitiated, growth style stocks are characterized by high monthly price momentum, strong revenue growth rates, steep valuations, high retention ratios, limited shareholder distributions, etc., and value style stocks are characterized by cheap valuations (be it on a price-to-earnings basis, price-to-book value basis, price-to-cash flow basis, etc.), high dividend yields and payouts, low revenue growth profiles, etc.

Investors who are also comfortable with forgoing frequent dividend distributions on a monthly or quarterly basis will like EWA, as it distributes only on a semi-annual basis.

Given the strong tilt towards financial stocks, it’s also fair to say that EWA could be seen as an imperfect proxy for those looking to bet on the interest rate policies of the Australian central bank.

What Are The Risks Associated With EWA?

Foreign exchange-related risks are often an underappreciated facet of international investing. Even though EWA is priced in USD, the stocks it holds are denominated in AUD. The Aussie dollar is often perceived to be a risk-on currency—a currency that flourishes when risk appetite in the financial markets is high and suffers when risk appetite is on the wane. And, when risk-on conditions are not present in the global markets, it tends to suffer. A decline in the AUD will be detrimental for holders of EWA, as the ETF does not hedge its portfolio. Thus, when the Aussie-denominated assets are converted to USD, holders would end up receiving less than what they would normally have received in local currency (AUD) terms.

Ostensibly, one of the reasons why US investors would shift to Australian stocks is because they’re assuming they are getting a bit of diversification away from domestic stocks. EWA may not be a suitable developed market alternative, as its beta over the last 3 years (relative to the S&P500) of over 1x suggests that it pretty much moves in tandem with the US markets. Note that all the other major developed market ETFs have beta readings (vs. the S&P500) that are much lower than EWA. For those not familiar, the beta tells you how sensitive a product’s movements are to the movement of a tracking index.

Another key risk is that EWA’s valuations appear to be quite pricey, particularly in light of the long-term earnings prospects of its holdings, and this may dampen further investor sentiment. As per Morningstar data, EWA is priced at an elevated price-to-earnings multiple of close to 20x, which represents a 5% premium over global markets and an even steeper premium of 35% over developed markets outside the US. Normally, when equities are priced at steep valuation multiples, it’s because they are poised to deliver a lucrative trajectory of earnings growth. However, EWA’s holdings are currently poised to deliver less than 7% earnings growth, which is 200-300 bps lower than the other two options.

Is There An Alternative Australian-Focused ETF To EWA?

Besides EWA, Aussie-focused investors are also afforded the opportunity of pursuing another passive ETF called the Franklin FTSE Australia ETF (FLAU).

FLAU is substantially smaller in scope (only $70 million of total AUM, which is a pittance compared to EWA’s $1.5 billion) and has a much shorter listing history of less than eight years, as opposed to nearly three decades for EWA. It tracks a different index to EWA called the FTSE Australia Capped Index.

The main difference to note is that FLAU’s portfolio is wider in scope, covering over 100 Australian stocks, but once again the onus is on giant caps, although a double-digit exposure to mid-caps is welcome (unlike EWA). FLAU also stands out for its remarkably cheap expense ratio of just 0.09%, which is not even one-fifth the cost of EWA.

Both products offer yields in excess of 3%, but the smaller ETF has the slight edge. Also note that both products don’t resort to a great deal of churn (as exemplified by an annual turnover rate of mid-single digits).

However, FLAU’s lack of popularity is very evident in the daily dollar volumes that it sees. It trades only around $200 thousand per day and is a long shot from what EWA trades daily. This ends up leaving an inimical mark on the average spreads that FLAU witnesses between its bid and the ask. An average spread of 0.74% is very concerning, whereas EWA’s is perfectly acceptable at just 0.03%.

Summary

EWA, which almost has a three-decade history of covering Australian giants and large caps, is currently the largest and most liquid Australian-focused ETF in the market. However, EWA is a pricey product (both in structure and the valuation of its holdings), has strong concentration effects, and may not serve as the most appropriate diversifier for those looking for developed market alternatives from the US markets.

This article answers these three main questions about EWA:

  • Which investor will find EWA appealing?
  • What are the risks associated with EWA?
  • How Does EWA Compare to FLAU, the other Australian-focused ETF?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the Fund for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about this ETF.

data.title: WTI Crude Forecast: Risk Premium Fades, Supply Pressures Mount, Bearish Trend Ahead
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

The geopolitical risk premium in the oil market has faded, taking a back seat after a four-week, 30% parabolic rally in West Texas Oil CFD (a proxy for WTI crude futures) during the initial phase of the Israel-Iran conflict.

US crude oil inventories are building up again

The growth of US crude oil inventories excluding the Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) on a year-on-year basis has an indirect correlation with the movement of WTI crude oil, as a build-up in oil inventories puts downside pressure on oil prices.

Since 20 June 2025, the drawn down of US crude oil inventories (excluding SPR) has slowed down from -9.9% y/y to -1.3% y/y as of 1 August 2025 based on data from the US Energy Information Administration (EIA) which suggests a potential build-up in oil inventories which is likely to put further downside pressure on the prices of WTI crude oil (see Fig. 1).

A possible reduction of US sanctions on Russian oil

Recent media reports have highlighted that the Russian government confirmed that Presidents Putin and Trump will meet for summit talks on ending the war in Ukraine in the next few days.

Hence, a ceasefire deal between Russia and Ukraine is likely to allow the removal or reduction of sanctions on Russia’s oil exports, in turn, increasing oil supply on top of ongoing OPEC+ production hikes where the cartel has agreed to pump an extra 2.5 million barrels of oil per day starting in September.

The net effect is a more dampening effect on the prices of WTI crude oil.

The three-month corrective rebound in WTI crude oil may have ended

The West Texas Oil CFD has broken below its 20-day, 50-day, and 200-day moving averages. In addition, its daily MACD trend indicator has broken below a former parallel ascending support from 6 May 2025 and continued to trend downwards below its centreline.

These observations suggest that a three-month corrective rebound from the 9 April 2025 low to the 23 June 2025 high is likely to have ended. The next possible movement of the West Texas Oil CFD is likely a medium-term (multi-week) impulsive bearish down move within a major downtrend phase in place since the 28 September 2023 high (see Fig. 2).

Bearish bias below US$67.25/68.80 key medium-term pivotal resistance for the next supports to come in at US$60.55, US$55.00, and US$50.50/49.10 (congestion area of 5 June/7 Aug 2017 & Fibonacci extension).

However, a clearance above US$68.80 invalidates the bearish scenario for a squeeze up to retest the next medium-term resistances at US$71.30 and US$74.00.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
